questions.question: How does the Shor code achieve protection against both X and Z errors?

options.text: Uses more qubits than needed for either error type
Concatenates bit-flip code (inner) with phase-flip code (outer)
Measures errors faster than they occur
Uses a different encoding for each error type

questions.explanation: The Shor code uses concatenation: the outer phase-flip code encodes |0⟩→|+++⟩, |1⟩→|---⟩, then each |+⟩ or |-⟩ is encoded with the inner bit-flip code. This gives protection against both error types.

questions.question: How many stabilizer generators does the [[9,1,3]] Shor code have?
questions.explanation: An [[n,k,d]] code has n-k independent stabilizer generators. For the Shor code, n=9 and k=1, so there are 9-1=8 generators: six Z-type (for X error detection within blocks) and two X-type (for Z error detection across blocks).

questions.question: What do the X-type stabilizers X₀X₁X₂X₃X₄X₅ and X₃X₄X₅X₆X₇X₈ detect?

options.text: Z errors that affect the relative phase between blocks

questions.explanation: The X-type stabilizers compare the phase between blocks. A Z error on any qubit in a block changes that block's sign (|+⟩↔|-⟩), which the X-type stabilizers detect as a change in parity between blocks.

questions.question: If a Y error occurs on qubit 4, what syndromes will be triggered?

options.text: Only Z-type syndromes in block B
Both Z-type syndromes in block B AND X-type syndromes

questions.explanation: Since Y = iXZ, a Y error triggers both the X-error detection (Z-type stabilizers Z₃Z₄ and Z₄Z₅ in block B) and the Z-error detection (X-type stabilizers). The intersection identifies the Y error location.

questions.question: What is the logical Z operator for the Shor code?
questions.explanation: The logical Z operator is Z₀Z₃Z₆ (or equivalently Z₁Z₄Z₇ or Z₂Z₅Z₈). It applies Z to one qubit in each block, changing the sign of all three blocks simultaneously, which flips the logical state.

questions.question: Why can't the Shor code correct two arbitrary errors?

options.text: The syndrome cannot distinguish 2-error patterns from 1-error patterns

questions.explanation: With distance d=3, some 2-error patterns produce the same syndrome as single errors. The decoder would apply the wrong correction, converting the 2 errors into 3 errors and causing a logical error.

questions.question: The Shor code is the most efficient code for correcting single arbitrary errors.
questions.explanation: False. The Shor code uses 9 qubits for [[9,1,3]]. The Steane code achieves the same [[7,1,3]] parameters with only 7 qubits, and the perfect [[5,1,3]] code uses just 5 qubits (the theoretical minimum for distance-3).
